text:
Arize AI was just named to the Agentic List 2026!

Presented by the AI Agent Conference and curated by @simonchannet at @FirsthandVC in partnership with NYSE Wired and @theCUBE, the award recognizes the top 120 agentic AI companies shaping the future through autonomous, intelligent systems that transform enterprise operations.

Alyx 2.0 is live.

An AI engineering agent built into Arize AX that can reason across multi-step workflows and execute autonomously.

↳ Error analysis
↳ Prompt experimentation
↳ Trace debugging

No more stitching everything together by hand.

Most AI assistants in dev tools follow one decision tree from the top. Alyx breaks down complex tasks, maintains context, and acts across your entire AI lifecycle.

text:
We just released a new Tracing tutorial for Arize AX: a practical walkthrough for instrumenting, inspecting, and evaluating your AI applications with end-to-end traces.

If you’re building agents/LLM apps, it’s hard to improve what you can’t see. This tutorial shows how to go from those black-box outputs to fully traceable, debuggable workflows. It covers:

🔍 Sending your first traces: Instrument your application and start capturing spans, inputs, outputs, and metadata in just a few steps

🧵 Understanding trace structure: Break down agents into spans for models, tools, retrieval, and custom logic so you can see exactly what happened

📝 Adding annotations & evaluations:  Layer in human feedback, code-based checks, or LLM evaluators directly on traces

🔄 Analyzing sessions end-to-end: Group traces into sessions to debug multi-step interactions and user journeys

Get started below ⬇️

Thanks to everyonewho came out to @joinstationf in Paris tonight to hear @dat_attacked, Jen Person, and JL de Morlhon and to our friends at @gokoyeb and @docker for organizing. https://t.co/NAWfKNUXAE

urls.title: Defining the Dataset That Powers Your Experiments - Arize AX Docs

text:
We just released a new Datasets & Experiments tutorial for Arize AX — a practical walkthrough for running structured experiments on your AI applications.

If you’re iterating on prompts, models, or agent logic, changes can feel “better” without actually being better. This tutorial shows how to move from guesswork to repeatable evaluation. It covers:

• Defining structured datasets 📊 - Create curated datasets from real examples so you can benchmark the behaviors that actually matter

• Running experiments with code-based evaluators 💻 - Add fast, deterministic checks against ground truth

• Running experiments with LLM-as-a-Judge 🧠 - Score outputs on more subjective criteria like helpfulness, actionability, or safety

• Building an iteration workflow 🔄 - Compare experiment runs, analyze results, and systematically validate changes before pushing to production.

If you’re building agents or LLM applications and want a disciplined way to test improvements, prevent regressions, and track quality over time, this tutorial walks through the full Arize AX workflow end to end!

Get started below ⬇️

At conversational forms pioneer @typeform, "evaluations are part of the product itself—not just a back-end task," notes Senior Data Scientist Marta Lorens. They're are also a team sport. ▶️ Learn about Typeform's approach to agent engineering and see how they leverage Arize AX and @awscloud: https://t.co/TzXKMcuL1i

Our next community reading will dive into "CVE-Bench: A Benchmark for AI Agents' Ability to Exploit Real-World Web Application Vulnerabilities" with authors @maxYuxuanZhu @ddkang of @UofIllinois RSVP: https://t.co/WU3GABjfU5 https://t.co/Rq1bVtfWSg

We just released a new Evaluations tutorial for Arize AX — get your evals up and running in minutes ✨

You can configure both LLM-as-a-Judge and code-based evaluators directly from the UI. Define your criteria, attach them to a project, and start scoring outputs right away. No heavy setup required!

Evaluators are reusable across projects, so once you define your quality standards (correctness, actionability, safety, etc.), you can apply them consistently wherever you need them.

If you're building agents or LLM agents and applications and want systematic quality checks, this walkthrough shows exactly how to set it up.

Get started below 👇

text:
New in Arize AX this week!

➡️ Programmatic prompt management: Full REST API coverage for prompts, including create, list, get, and delete endpoints. Build and version prompts systematically with reusability across projects.

➡️ Improved trace debugging: Enhanced span filtering API lets you query trace data with complex conditions to find specific LLM calls, tool invocations, or retrieval operations. The span table now supports click-to-open details and bulk selection. Experiment runs link directly to their full observability traces.

➡️ Developer experience updates: Escaped curly braces in eval templates so you can include JSON examples without triggering variable errors. Playground sessions automatically capture traces for visibility into your experimentation workflow.

Try it out! https://t.co/3Mgn8vGga1

"We needed to see the traces in a hierarchical format—within a single run, what prompts are we sending, what tool calls are being invoked, and is the output structure correct? This depth is essential for building the high-performance operations our customers expect."

Thomas Goodman, Principal Software Development Engineer, @CVSHealth, explains the importance of AI-specialized observability. Watch the full interview: https://t.co/zLnz3DRfwH

text:
We’re excited to announce the release of our best Python SDK to date! This SDK makes it possible to have workflows that are consistent, easily configurable, and optimized for iteration.

Here’s what’s new:

➡️ A single ArizeClient for all workflows — no more dealing with multiple clients that operate differently

➡️ A consistent client.<resource>.<action> interface that makes all capabilities easy to discover and use

➡️ A customizable experience with standardized arguments configurable in code or via environment variables

If you’re tracing, evaluating, or experimenting with AI applications and agents, our Python SDK helps streamline your workflow.

Get started in the docs & check out the tutorial below for a full walkthrough ⬇️

text:
Eval Hub wasn't the only feature shipped in Arize AX this week! Check out some other updates:

➡️ Custom Metrics now support LIKE and ILIKE operators, enabling more flexible text pattern matching that LLMs naturally reach for when building queries

➡️ Comprehensive RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) rollout across the platform, including custom role assignment in the UI, SAML role mapping support, and granular permissions for projects, datasets, experiments, evaluators, prompts, dashboards, and monitors - giving enterprise teams fine-grained control over data access and security

➡️ New pivot table widget support added to dashboards, enabling summarized tabular data views with grouped dimensions and configurable aggregations

➡️ Synthetic data generation expanded - you can now create synthetic data from single trace views and append synthetic spans to existing datasets, with intelligent column selection for better performance

➡️ Trace slideover now resizable for better visibility into detailed trace information

And much more!
Learn more: https://t.co/7y3fmOSkpc

text:
RT @arizeai: New in Arize AX: Evaluator Hub 🚀

Evaluator Hub introduces reusable, versioned evaluators that can be shared across evaluation…

retweet.original_text:
New in Arize AX: Evaluator Hub 🚀

Evaluator Hub introduces reusable, versioned evaluators that can be shared across evaluation tasks, instead of being redefined per task.

This makes evaluation behavior easier to reason about as tasks, datasets, and models evolve.

What this unlocks: 
🔹Reuse the same evaluator definition across tasks to reduce eval drift
🔹Scope LLM configuration to the evaluator for consistent behavior
🔹Track changes with version history and commit messages 
🔹Reuse evaluators across datasets using column mappings

Try it out today!

urls.title: Prompt Hub - Arize AX Docs

text:
🚀 New in Arize AX: Custom Prompt Release Labels

Your prompts always don’t fit neatly into dev / staging / prod.

In reality, you’re running experiments in parallel, testing variants, shipping feature-specific prompts, and iterating fast based on real user behavior.

With Custom Prompt Release Labels, you can now tag prompts however your system actually works — experiments, versions, customers, features, or anything else.

Why this matters:
• Cleaner comparisons between prompt variants
• Faster debugging and safer rollbacks
• Smoother integration into your system when prompts change

We hope that prompt versioning adapts to the developer and gives you full control! ✍️

We’ve added OpenInference instrumentation for Pipecat using OpenTelemetry. Pipecat services can now emit standardized traces and spans with semantic context across realtime agent pipelines.

The integration instruments:
🔹agent execution
🔹tool / function calls
🔹model interactions
🔹service and pipeline boundaries

Traces follow OpenInference conventions and can be inspected in @ArizePhoenix  for debugging, performance analysis, and understanding execution flow in production.

It was another super busy week! Here's what we shipped this week in Arize AX:

➡️ Tracing table loads 30-50% faster with truncated strings that lazy load on hover, significantly improving performance for large traces while maintaining full data visibility when you need it
➡️ Expand traces directly in the tracing table to view child spans and understand request branching without leaving the main view, making it easier to navigate complex execution paths
➡️ Custom release labels for prompt versions let you tag and track specific prompt iterations like staging or production releases, bringing software engineering best practices to prompt management
➡️ Eval Hub now displays which LLM model (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, etc.) was used for each evaluator and shows evaluator counts in running tasks, providing better visibility into evaluation configurations
➡️ Add spans and dataset examples to labeling queues directly from the spans table, trace slideover, or queue records table, making it easier to route data for human review and feedback
➡️ RBAC enhancements with new permission system for space access controls for more granular security management

Chewy, a trusted online destination for pet parents and partners, is an early adopter of breakthroughs like AI-powered checkout. With Arize AX, AI engineers at Chewy can rely on end-to-end tracing across agent flows, alerting, and more. See AX up close: https://t.co/J0EITw1fLm https://t.co/mTxxi8c1SQ

text:
RT @ArizePhoenix: We just released a new Python Tracing Quickstart for Phoenix!

It’s all about learning the full workflows that turn AI ag…

retweet.original_text:
We just released a new Python Tracing Quickstart for Phoenix!

It’s all about learning the full workflows that turn AI agents into systems you can actually understand and assess.

Agents don’t fail like traditional software. They can misclassify, retrieve the wrong docs, forget context, or call tools with bad inputs. Console logs won’t save you.

In this walkthrough, you build a real agent and instrument it as you go:

➡️ First: trace everything in minutes. Every LLM call, tool execution, and RAG retrieval is grouped into a single execution flow you can click through. No guessing why something broke.

➡️ Then: go beyond “it ran” to “it worked.” Attach human feedback, capture user reactions, and run LLM-as-judge evals so quality becomes a metric, not a vibe. Patterns emerge across hundreds of traces.

➡️ Finally: make conversation observable. Trace state, memory, and context across turns with sessions, so you can measure goal completion and pinpoint where context gets lost.

By the end, you’ll know exactly what happened, why it happened, and what to fix next — using real AI observability workflows, not ad hoc debugging.

A TS version is also available 💫

Docs to get started are below! 👇

text:
RT @ArizePhoenix: 🔍 New Phoenix Evaluation Integration: @CVSHealth’s UQLM (Uncertainty Quantification for Language Models) metric.

UQLM gi…

retweet.original_text:
🔍 New Phoenix Evaluation Integration: @CVSHealth’s UQLM (Uncertainty Quantification for Language Models) metric.

UQLM gives you a quantitative measure of LLM uncertainty, so you can:

⚪️ Spot hallucination-prone responses by surfacing low-confidence outputs
⚪️ Flag uncertain generations for fallback, review, or guardrails
⚪️ Compare prompts & models more rigorously with uncertainty signals

text:
RT @ArizePhoenix: In our latest Evals Series webinar, we covered how to evaluate your evaluator (“aligning the judge”).

AI development has…

retweet.original_text:
In our latest Evals Series webinar, we covered how to evaluate your evaluator (“aligning the judge”).

AI development has two loops: improving the application and improving the evals themselves. Meta-evaluation lives in the inner loop.

Since LLM judges scale human annotation (rather than deterministic metrics), validating them like classifiers is essential.

We also walked through a live demo of this loop in practice, iteratively improving the judge and showing measurable gains at each step.

Curious how others are validating & improving their evaluators.

text:
In case you missed it: we gathered with builders in NYC last night to dive into what really moves the needle after an agent is deployed.

A lot of progress starts in production. This means learning from how your agent behaves in the wild and feeding those findings back into your development pipelines.

We walked through how continuous evaluation surfaces failures, closes the feedback loop, and turns real insights into a better performing agent.

With Arize AX, online evals and real-time tracing makes every production run part of an iterative cycle. This helps teams ship smarter agents that are consistently evolving.

Check out the video recording of the session: https://t.co/lG4bdXoERF

text:
RT @ArizePhoenix: 📚 Just dropped: new end-to-end Phoenix tutorials for Python and TypeScript!

Improving an agent starts with understanding…

retweet.original_text:
📚 Just dropped: new end-to-end Phoenix tutorials for Python and TypeScript!

Improving an agent starts with understanding where & why it fails. That requires careful error analysis, clear task definitions, and a repeatable iteration loop.

These new tutorials walk through the core workflow for building and improving LLM applications with Phoenix. Learn about:

➡️ Interpreting and comparing agent traces

➡️ Defining tasks and creating datasets for structured experimentation

➡️ Writing purposeful LLM evals to measure performance and uncover failure modes

➡️ Iterating based on evaluation results and human feedback to improve reliability

By working through this cycle, you’ll develop an systematic approach to deploying agents that perform consistently and handle edge cases with confidence.

Choose the language you work in and get started below ⬇️

text:
Our LLM-as-a-Judge 101 virtual workshop was so popular, we're returning for LLM-as-a-Judge 102. 🎓 RSVP: https://t.co/fDzfSK3RWX

In this session focused on meta-evaluation, you’ll learn how to validate whether your LLM judge is measuring the right thing, how closely it aligns with human judgment, and how to identify where it fails. We’ll walk through comparing LLM and human annotations on a curated golden dataset, calculating precision/recall/F1, and inspecting disagreement cases to understand why your evaluator struggles.

We’ll also cover advanced techniques such as treating humans and LLMs as annotators to estimate inter-annotator agreement, and using high-temperature stress tests to detect prompt ambiguity or unstable reasoning.

See you soon!

urls.title: LLM-as-a-Judge 102: Meta Evaluation · Zoom · Luma

text:
If you've been debugging agentic LLM apps by scrolling through hundreds of spans, Arize AX can help you do better!

Agent Graph gives you a node-based visual map of your agent workflows, so you can instantly see execution paths, identify failure points, spot self-looping behavior, and understand which agents are calling which.

Think of it as a "logical flow map" instead of digging through trace details one by one.

Built-in support for LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI, OpenAI Agents, and Agno. For custom agents or hybrid setups, just add two span attributes to the components you want visualized.

Learn more in our docs:
https://t.co/MvXi2iyQFk

Arize AX Monitors let you set up alerts for the metrics that actually matter in your LLM apps: latency spikes, hallucination rates, eval failures, error counts, token usage, you name it.

You've got two paths:
➡️ Custom monitors for tracking exactly what you care about (like "alert me if % hallucinated goes above 10%")
➡️ Managed monitors for one-click setup on the basics like latency and error count.

Everything is threshold-based, so you get notified the moment something drifts outside acceptable ranges. Great for catching issues before your users do.

Learn more:
https://t.co/HKt364JghS

text:
Learn how to build better AI applications with this introduction to LLM-as-a-judge evaluation!

Key takeaways:
➡️ Start by observing real application data to understand failure modes before defining metrics.
➡️ Keep metrics specific, answerable, and actionable - avoid catch-all "quality" scores or having 10+ overlapping metrics.
➡️ When writing judge prompts, treat them like human annotation guidelines: keep context under 4K tokens, and don't overthink fancy prompting techniques.
➡️ Most importantly, create a golden dataset with human annotations to measure judge alignment through meta-evaluation.

Elizabeth Hutton emphasizes that imperfect evals beat no evals, and this is an iterative process with two overlapping loops - one improving your application, another improving your evaluations. She also covers when to use code vs human vs LLM evals, explains pairwise vs direct scoring approaches, and shares practical pitfalls to avoid.

Check out the full video:
https://t.co/3Yvl87xGho

Check our our blog post that goes more in-depth into Prompt Learning and GEPA: https://t.co/XNoQ6M2sxp
